pathophysiology_summary: Dyspepsia is a symptom complex of epigastric pain/burning, postprandial fullness and early satiety, arising from acid sensitivity, gastric accommodation/motility disturbance and visceral hypersensitivity; many cases are functional or acid-related, and Helicobacter pylori is a common contributor. In a patient under 60 without alarm features, guidelines support H. pylori test-and-treat and/or an empirical PPI trial with lifestyle measures rather than immediate endoscopy. The ground-truth tasks are (1) screening GI alarm features that mandate gastroscopy (dysphagia/odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding/anaemia, persistent vomiting, epigastric mass, age >60 with new dyspepsia), (2) keeping an appropriate cardiac and biliary safety-net (epigastric pain cannot fully exclude atypical ACS or biliary disease remotely — though the meal-related, reproducible, non-exertional pattern favours dyspepsia), and (3) rational management: PPI + lifestyle + H. pylori testing, avoiding NSAIDs.

differential.evidence_basis: Postprandial epigastric burning/fullness/early satiety with partial antacid relief.
Common cause; H. pylori test-and-treat appropriate.
Epigastric pain can be cardiac — screen exertional/radiation/diaphoresis features.
Screen RUQ radiation, Murphy's, fever — absent here.
Alarm features (weight loss, dysphagia, bleeding, age>60) would mandate endoscopy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Dyspepsia may lead, but alarm features, ACS and biliary disease must be actively screened before empirical treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: No alarm features, non-exertional meal-related pattern reproducible on palpation, no NSAID use, age <60 — uninvestigated dyspepsia suitable for test-and-treat / empirical PPI.
Lowered but not excluded remotely; retained as a cardiac safety-net for any exertional/radiating features.
No alarm features; escalation to endoscopy if they develop or symptoms persist.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative alarm/cardiac screen, dyspepsia leads; PPI + lifestyle + H. pylori test, with GI-bleed and cardiac safety-nets.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; reassess/endoscopy if alarm features, refractory or age>60.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is uninvestigated dyspepsia managed empirically.

red_flags.description: GI alarm features — dysphagia/odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding (haematemesis/melaena)/anaemia, persistent vomiting, epigastric mass, new dyspepsia age >60
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mandate gastroscopy to exclude malignancy/ulcer complications.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of upper-GI malignancy or bleeding ulcer.

red_flags.description: Cardiac features — exertional trigger, radiation to arm/jaw, diaphoresis, dyspnoea
red_flags.clinical_significance: Epigastric pain can be atypical ACS; cannot be excluded remotely.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing a PPI without excluding dysphagia/weight loss/bleeding that require endoscopy.
Assuming epigastric pain is benign without a cardiac/biliary safety-net.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Empirical PPI + lifestyle + arrange H. pylori testing; ED for GI-bleed features; 000/ECG for cardiac features; endoscopy if alarm features/refractory.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing malignancy alarm features or atypical ACS.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For uninvestigated dyspepsia without alarm features in patients <60: H. pylori test-and-treat and/or empirical PPI with lifestyle measures; avoid NSAIDs. Endoscopy for alarm features, age >60 with new symptoms, or refractory disease.
